title: How Much Life Insurance Do I Need in Canada?

secondary_keywords: how much life insurance do i need canada

seo_keywords.real_example_to_use: Jennifer, 36, Mississauga, 2 kids, $85K income, $550K mortgage, $30K car/student debt. DIME = $1.35M. She bought $1.25M 20-year term for $82/month.

page_data.heading: How Much Life Insurance Do You Actually Need?
page_data.subheading: Too little leaves your family short. Too much wastes money. This guide shows you exactly how to calculate the right number for your situation.

_writer_notes.focus: Frame the problem: people don't know how much to get, and the answer varies by situation. Position this page as the trusted guide.
key_points: No single answer — depends on income, debts, family size, existing coverage
Two methods: DIME (detailed) and income multiplier (quick estimate)

page_data.heading: Why Getting the Amount Right Matters

_writer_notes.focus: Quick motivation section. Two dangers: under-insured (family struggles) and over-insured (wasted premiums).
key_points: Under-insured: family can't maintain lifestyle, may lose home or struggle with debt
Over-insured: paying for more coverage than needed — money that could go to savings or RRSPs
CLHIA data: 56% of life insurance policyholders in Canada may be under-insured
The sweet spot: covers all major financial obligations + income replacement for dependents

_writer_notes.focus: Explain the quick 10x rule and why it's a starting point, not a final answer.
key_points: Simple rule: annual income × 10. If you earn $80K, get $800K in coverage.
Adjustments: fewer dependents → lower multiplier. Young children + large mortgage → higher multiplier.
Better multipliers by situation: no-mortgage renter with no kids = 5–7x; mortgage + 2 kids = 10–15x
Limitation: ignores specific debts, mortgage size, spouse income, and education needs
_writer_notes.structure: Explain the rule. Quick table: income level × multiplier = coverage estimate. Then note limitations.

page_data.heading: Method 2: The DIME Method (Complete Needs Analysis)

_writer_notes.focus: Full DIME breakdown. Most authoritative method — used by licensed advisors.
key_points: D = Debt: total non-mortgage debts (credit cards, car loans, student loans, personal debt)
I = Income: annual income × number of years family needs income replacement (usually 10–20 years)
M = Mortgage: outstanding mortgage balance
E = Education: estimated post-secondary costs for each child ($40–60K per child in Canada)
Formula: D + I + M + E = Total Gross Need
Then subtract: existing life insurance coverage from all sources (employer group, existing policy)
Result = your coverage gap — the amount of new insurance you need
_writer_notes.real_example: Jennifer, 36, Mississauga: D=$30K + I=($85K × 15 years = $1.275M) + M=$550K + E=($50K × 2 kids = $100K) = $1.955M gross. Minus $100K group life from employer = $1.855M. She rounded to $1.25M as a realistic target within budget.
_writer_notes.structure: Acronym breakdown with bullet for each letter → formula summary → Jennifer example worked out step by step.

page_data.heading: How Much Do You Need? By Life Stage

_writer_notes.focus: Segmented guidance for common Canadian life situations.
key_points: Single, no dependents: $100K–$300K (covers debts, funeral, small gift to parents/partner)
Married, no kids, both working: 5–7x each income (covers mortgage if one dies)
Married with young children: 10–15x income for primary earner; 5–7x for secondary earner
Single parent: 15x income minimum — sole earner, full childcare costs on one policy
Married, kids grown, mortgage almost done: 3–5x income — most debts gone, kids independent
Retired, no mortgage: $100–$200K for final expenses, estate equalization, legacy
_writer_notes.structure: 6 life stage profiles in a clean table. Life stage / Coverage range / Rationale.

page_data.heading: 5 Things People Forget When Calculating Coverage

_writer_notes.focus: Quick, scannable list of overlooked factors.
key_points: Childcare costs: if primary caregiver dies, surviving parent may need $2,000–$4,000/month for childcare in Ontario
Inflation: income replacement in year 15 buys less than today — consider a COLA rider or higher initial coverage
Group coverage isn't portable: employer DI or group life disappears if you change jobs
Spouse income uncertainty: don't assume spouse will work full-time immediately — recovery takes time
Business debts: if self-employed, include business liabilities in your coverage calculation

_writer_notes.focus: Bridge to calculator page or embedded mini-calculator. Keep copy short — get them to the tool.
key_points: Fill in 6 fields → instant DIME estimate
No email required
Then compare quotes for that exact coverage amount
_writer_notes.callout_after: Blue box: 'Not sure what inputs to use? Use the defaults we've pre-filled for an average Ontario family with 2 children and a $500K mortgage.'

faqs.question: Is $500,000 enough life insurance in Canada?
faqs.answer: For some Canadians, yes — but for many it's not enough. If you have a $600,000 mortgage, two young children, and earn $80,000/year, $500,000 would leave your family significantly under-protected. Run the DIME calculation with your own numbers to find out if $500K covers your needs.
faqs._guidance: Real answer with specifics. Don't just say 'it depends' — illustrate with numbers.

faqs.question: Can I have too much life insurance?
faqs.answer: Technically unlimited coverage is available, but most insurers cap coverage at a multiple of your income to prevent over-insurance. From a practical standpoint, there's little financial reason to carry more than your DIME total. Over-insuring means higher premiums that could be better spent on savings or investments.
faqs._guidance: Practical answer. Note that insurers do impose limits.

faqs.question: Does my spouse need life insurance if they don't work?
faqs.answer: Yes. A stay-at-home parent provides substantial economic value — childcare, home management, and support that would cost $40,000–$80,000/year to replace. If your spouse died, you'd need coverage to pay for those services while maintaining your career.
faqs._guidance: This is a commonly overlooked gap. Give the economic value framing.

faqs.question: Should I include my group life insurance from work?
faqs.answer: Yes, but with caution. Group life insurance from your employer typically covers 1–2x your annual salary and is not portable — if you leave your job, coverage ends. Include it in your coverage gap calculation, but consider whether you'd still be covered if you changed employers.
faqs._guidance: Acknowledge group coverage but flag the portability risk.
